title: Gaussian Mathematics: Attributes, Covariance, and Spherical Harmonics

learningObjectives.description: List the 59 parameters of a single 3D Gaussian by category (position, rotation, scale, opacity, SH coefficients) and identify which category dominates the storage budget
Derive the covariance factorization Σ = RSS⊤R⊤ and explain why this parameterization guarantees positive semi-definiteness throughout gradient-based optimization

Explain how spherical harmonics decompose view-dependent color into basis functions, identify which SH orders encode the DC color vs. view-dependent highlights, and state why the AC coefficients dominate the parameter budget
Compute the total uncompressed scene size in MB given a Gaussian count and float32 parameter budget, and identify which attribute categories offer the most leverage for reduction

content:
## The 59-Parameter Gaussian

Every 3D Gaussian splat carries a fixed set of attributes. Understanding what each encodes and how many parameters it contributes is essential for any compression discussion.

The full parameter budget per Gaussian:

| Attribute | Parameters | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Position \(\mu\) | 3 | Location in world space \((x,y,z)\) |
| Scale \(s\) | 3 | Anisotropic extent along each axis |
| Rotation \(q\) | 4 | Quaternion encoding orientation |
| Opacity \(\sigma_\alpha\) | 1 | Base transparency (pre-sigmoid) |
| DC color (SH order 0) | 3 | View-independent RGB color |
| SH coefficients (orders 1–3) | 45 | View-dependent color variation |
| **Total** | **59** | |

At float32 precision: **59 × 4 = 236 bytes per Gaussian**. A typical scene with 5 million Gaussians is ~1.2 GB.

## Covariance Factorization: Why \(\Sigma = RSS^\top R^\top\)

A covariance matrix must be **positive semi-definite (PSD)** — this is a mathematical requirement for a valid Gaussian. Storing and optimizing a full 3×3 symmetric matrix is possible, but gradient descent will almost certainly produce non-PSD matrices at intermediate steps, breaking the Gaussian interpretation.

3DGS avoids this by factoring:

\[\Sigma = R S S^\top R^\top\]

where:
- \(R \in SO(3)\) is a rotation matrix (encoded as a quaternion \(q \in \mathbb{R}^4\), \(\|q\|=1\))
- \(S = \text{diag}(s_1, s_2, s_3)\) is a diagonal scale matrix with \(s_i > 0\)

**Why is \(\Sigma\) always PSD?** For any vector \(v\):
\[v^\top \Sigma v = v^\top R S S^\top R^\top v = \|S^\top R^\top v\|^2 \geq 0\]

This is always non-negative by construction. The optimization landscape is unconstrained — you can apply gradient descent to \(q\) and \(\log s_i\) (log to keep scale positive) without special projection steps.

The geometric interpretation: \(S\) stretches a unit sphere along three principal axes, and \(R\) rotates the result. The Gaussian ellipsoid is the image of this transformation.

## Spherical Harmonics for View-Dependent Color

Real surfaces exhibit view-dependent appearance: specular highlights, reflections, refractions, and subsurface scattering all change color based on the viewing direction. A single RGB color per Gaussian is insufficient.

**Spherical harmonics (SH)** are an orthonormal basis of functions on the unit sphere. They decompose a directional function \(f(\theta, \phi)\) into frequency bands:

\[f(\theta,\phi) = \sum_{l=0}^{L} \sum_{m=-l}^{l} c_l^m Y_l^m(\theta,\phi)\]

where \(Y_l^m\) are the SH basis functions and \(c_l^m\) are learnable coefficients.

The number of coefficients per band:
- Order 0 (DC): \((0+1)^2 = 1\) coefficient → view-independent color
- Order 1: \((1+1)^2 = 4\) total → 3 new coefficients (linear variation)
- Order 2: 9 total → 5 new coefficients
- Order 3: \((3+1)^2 = 16\) total → 7 new coefficients

Since 3DGS uses order 3 and stores **one SH expansion per color channel**, that's \(16 \times 3 = 48\) SH parameters. Subtracting the DC term (already counted under "DC color"): **45 SH AC coefficients**.

### Why SH Dominates the Bit Budget

The 45 SH coefficients represent \(45/59 \approx 76\%\) of all parameters per Gaussian. This immediately suggests SH degree reduction as the highest-leverage compaction strategy — a topic we will explore in Module 3.

The trade-off: dropping from order 3 to order 0 (DC only) halves the parameter count but eliminates all view-dependent effects. Intermediate degrees offer a smooth quality–size curve.

## The Gaussian Density Function in Context

Putting it together, the full density contribution of Gaussian \(i\) at point \(\mathbf{p}\):

\[f_i(\mathbf{p}) = \sigma_\alpha \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{p}-\mu_i)^\top \Sigma_i^{-1}(\mathbf{p}-\mu_i)\right)\]

The color seen along direction \(\mathbf{d}\) is then:

\[c_i(\mathbf{d}) = \text{sigmoid}\!\left(\sum_{l,m} c_l^m Y_l^m(\mathbf{d})\right)_{\text{per channel}}\]

The sigmoid maps the linear SH sum into \([0,1]\) for each of the three RGB channels.

references.label: Kerbl et al. 2023
references.title: 3D Gaussian Splatting for Real-Time Radiance Field Rendering

references.label: Bagdasarian et al. 2025
references.title: 3DGS.zip: A Survey on 3D Gaussian Splatting Compression Methods

terms.name: Covariance matrix
terms.desc: 3×3 symmetric matrix encoding the Gaussian's shape, size, and orientation. Must be positive semi-definite (PSD) — negative eigenvalues have no geometric meaning.

terms.name: Rotation matrix
terms.desc: Derived from the stored unit quaternion. Encodes how the Gaussian's principal axes are oriented in 3D world space.

terms.name: Scale matrix
terms.desc: Diagonal matrix with entries exp(s₀), exp(s₁), exp(s₂). The exponential keeps all scales strictly positive regardless of what the optimizer does to the raw parameters.

terms.name: Squared scales
terms.desc: Produces diag(s₀², s₁², s₂²) — the variance along each principal axis before the rotation is applied.

terms.name: Rotated covariance
terms.desc: R^⊤ transforms a world-space vector into the ellipsoid's local frame; S S^⊤ scales it; R rotates back. The result is the axis-aligned ellipsoid expressed in world coordinates.

equationBreakdowns.label: Why the Covariance Is Always Valid (PSD Proof)

terms.name: Quadratic form
terms.desc: A matrix is positive semi-definite if this expression is ≥ 0 for every vector v. PSD is the mathematical requirement for Σ to be a valid covariance matrix.

terms.name: Transformed vector
terms.desc: The input vector v rotated into the ellipsoid frame and scaled. R^⊤ is a rotation (orthonormal), S^⊤ = S is diagonal positive — both preserve the vector's existence.

terms.name: Squared Euclidean norm
terms.desc: A squared length is always ≥ 0 by definition. This is the key insight: by rewriting the quadratic form as a squared norm, PSD is guaranteed structurally — no clamping or projection needed during training.

terms.name: Directional function
terms.desc: The color (or any quantity) to represent as a function of viewing direction on the unit sphere. For 3DGS this is the RGB color seen from direction (θ, φ).

terms.name: Sum over bands
terms.desc: l is the frequency band (0 = constant, 1 = linear, 2 = quadratic, …). Higher l captures finer directional variation. 3DGS uses up to degree 3 (16 basis functions per color channel).

terms.name: SH coefficients
terms.desc: Learned scalar weights, one per (l, m) pair. These are the stored parameters — optimized by gradient descent to best reproduce the observed color from all training views.

terms.name: SH basis functions
terms.desc: Fixed, pre-computed functions on the sphere — the spherical analogue of Fourier sinusoids. Orthogonal: each captures a distinct directional frequency and can be evaluated with a few arithmetic ops.

terms.name: Density at point p
terms.desc: Combined contribution of Gaussian i at screen position p — opacity times geometric falloff. This is what gets alpha-composited in depth order to produce the final pixel color.

terms.name: Learned opacity
terms.desc: Scalar in (0, 1) after sigmoid activation. Independent of geometry — two Gaussians with identical shapes can have very different opacity values.

terms.name: Center (mean)
terms.desc: The 3D position of Gaussian i, optimized during training. Initialized from the SfM sparse point cloud; updated freely by gradient descent and Adaptive Density Control.

terms.name: Precision matrix
terms.desc: Inverse covariance — encodes how quickly influence falls off in each direction. A tight Gaussian (small σ) has a large precision; a broad Gaussian has a small one.

terms.name: Mahalanobis exponent
terms.desc: Shape-aware distance from p to the Gaussian center. Equals 0 at p = μ_i (maximum influence). Grows negative as p moves away, exponentially suppressing influence — with rate controlled by Σ.

terms.name: View-dependent color
terms.desc: RGB color Gaussian i contributes when viewed from direction d. Different viewing angles yield different colors — enabling specular highlights and other non-Lambertian effects.

terms.name: SH evaluation
terms.desc: Dot product of stored coefficients with basis functions evaluated at direction d. Computationally cheap — a handful of multiplications and additions, no MLP forward pass.

terms.name: Color activation
terms.desc: Maps the unbounded linear SH sum into [0, 1], producing a valid RGB value. Equivalent to 1 / (1 + exp(−x)). Applied independently to each channel.
